speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number two, Mr. Walter Absil. Mr. Absil, we were talking about how Hitler came to power. And I asked you whether it affected your life in Austria. When did you actually notice changes after 1933?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it really only affected us in 1938 when they made-- don't forget that the government in Austria was anti-Nazi. And they locked them up for-- you say 1933-- all the riots. And all the, the Nazis really achieved was getting locked up. Eventually-- my wife must have told you-- they killed Schuschnigg-- Dollfuss, not Schuschnigg. They killed Dollfuss. And there again, the people that killed them were hung. Maybe not all of them, but some of them got the death penalty.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was kind of reassuring for us that the, the Nazis were really the victims at that point in time. But then, of course, when the Anschluss came about. That's when we realized that we were really in danger. And I have to admit that my dad probably realized that faster than other people and-- by meeting someone on the street that told him that this folly can't last. Why don't you get out of here?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we managed by, by accident to obtain a visa for Belgium. It just so happened that my uncle, who was supposed to have immigrated to South America in 1936, met this girl in Belgium and got married. And the birth of his first child was right then and there in March 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my grandmother had gone to visit and help with the baby so that we placed a phone call to Belgium and said, well, how his mother? And says, what do you mean, how is mother? So anyway, finally, they made him understand that she was very sick. And she had been rushed to the hospital, was of course wasn't true.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they sent us a telegram. Come immediately. Mother in the hospital, very sick. With this telegram, we went to the Belgium embassy. And we obtained the visa for the family. And that's how immediately we left Austria. We left our apartment and all the merchandise and all the receivables behind and traveled with two little suitcases. I left with my aunt, her two boys, and my sister, and my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you leave before the Anschluss or after?

speaker: interviewee
text: We left after the Anschluss but before German troops had, had reached Vienna. As a matter of fact, the night in the train when we drove through Linz, I saw the German columns driving towards Vienna. So we left immediately.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you remember the Anschluss?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, like I said, what them all we remember is pack whatever you can in a little suitcase. And I took some of my stamp collection along. And I took some of my, my grandfather's silver boxes. And that was about it. The bike stayed behind and everything else. And we, we went to the train. And being three, four children, and my cousin and myself, we had a two trombeniks on the train. And we were only full of mischief.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents explain to you what the Anschluss was all about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in so many words, no. My, my dad stayed behind. And my uncle stayed behind. And that night, both of them slept. My uncle came to sleep in our apartment. And the Nazis raided his apartment. Right there and then, they would have been arrested had they slept in the wroong apartment. And when they saw that, that the Nazis trashed and robbed my uncle's apartment, they left the next day via Italy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did they stay behind?

speaker: interviewee
text: They figured that they had to straighten out their affairs, I guess. They sent the children and, and, and the women out of the country. And they themselves, I guess, had some things to straighten out. But they didn't. They left the next day.

speaker: interviewer
text: They had never been arrested in Austria? Never. What happened to Jews who did get arrested in Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the ones that got arrested in Austria, first of all, they dragged retail stores-- they dragged them out of their stores. They made them wash the floor on the street. Today in the center of Vienna, there's a monument that shows a Jew washing with a brush, washing the floor. I'm afraid to tell you that the dogs like that little monument very much. Also, the tourists sit on it. And the Viennese put some barbed wire on the poor fellow's back in order to deter tourists from sitting on his back. But the dogs still kind of do their business on it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that the Austrians initially were against the Nazis.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Austrian government.

speaker: interviewer
text: But not the Austrian people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not the Austrian people, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you suppose the Austrians hated Jews so much?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you have different reasons for not only the Austrians, but I would say the Catholic Central European has been nourished by the tales of the Jew killer for centuries. Only recently, I believe it was in 1956, has the Vatican made a declaration that is called nostra aetate, which states that the Jews of then, 2,000 years ago, and the Jews of today are not responsible for the sufferings of Jesus. But it took an Auschwitz for the Vatican to make such a declaration.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is one part of it. The Jew was always being used as a scapegoat. When 20 million Europeans died of the pest, the Jews had poisoned, poisoned the wells. We-- everybody knew that it was the Jews' fault. And what did you do? You killed the Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: When a raped or murdered girl or child was found anywhere in the woods-- today we know that anybody can be a rapist, a murderer-- it was the Jew that did it. Because they needed that child for ritual murders. This is the famous blood libel that Jews-- the fact that we eat kosher and that the blood is being removed from animals before a Jew would eat it was misunderstood by the Christians. And they believed that, that, that, that blood that we remove from animals is being used in a religious ceremony and that we used the blood of children to do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any such cases in Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were-- cases like that primarily were in the countryside. Somehow, I believe that in a big city, the police wouldn't believe such stories. I'm not talking about the Middle Ages. I'm talking about my lifetime. But in the countryside to this day, there's a village in Austria that believes that, that is praying to a child that was murdered. And they all know the Jews did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the name of this village?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. But it's been documented quite often, that little village that still believe that this child was murdered by the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you remember the name of the child?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I do not. But I've seen this in documentaries and that. And this little village, I couldn't tell you the name of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're at the train station now, going to Belgium.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Without your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you remember the exact date when you left Austria?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was, it was-- it was probably Sunday, the 15th of March, 1938. The Anschluss-- on, on the 13th of March, Austria was supposed to have an election for or against. It's funny that they had a referendum, just like the one in, in Quebec. And the answer was yes or no for Austria to become to stay independent or join the Reich. And this election or referendum was interrupted by the, the attack of the German army.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take for you to get to Belgium?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, we were-- we were stopped at the Austrian-Swiss border. And even though we still had Austrian customs agents at the border, we were, we were really searched. And I remember a fellow taking a pair of scissors from my, my mother's necessary and kind of stirring in, in her cold cream. I found that very funny that he would pull-- take the scissors and dump the scissors in the cold cream. Of course, he was looking for diamonds or whatever. And then we were again stopped in Basel in Switzerland, where overnight, France had declared that Austrian subjects now needed a visa in order to enter France. Up to that point in time, you didn't need one. So we have to stay over in Basel, [INAUDIBLE], and you know, and sleep there. And the next morning, my mother probably went to the French legation and obtained transit visas for us. With the Belgian visa, we could transit to France. So the next day only, we, we reached Belgium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where Where-- what, what was your destination in Belgium?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my uncle had a tiny house, a tiny little house there, with his wife and his baby, a newborn baby. And my grandmother already resided in that little house. And now imagine that-- two more women with four more children moved into that little house. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this, which city?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Brussels, in Brussels. And I remember sleeping on the floor, because we run out of bed space, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did your father join you?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few days later, via Italy. He had been able to, again, transit France come to Belgium. And then we rented a little apartment somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was he able to support his family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there, by hook and by crook, you, you tried everything. You, you put ads in the paper, selling. Because you couldn't-- you didn't have a work permit. I would say also that the Jewish Committee of, of Brussels helped us. I think we got a certain amount per week to-- just to subside, and then whatever you found. I remember that my dad was buying used strong boxes and had them refurnished and resold them. We had them repainted. And you tried anything at all in order to make a living. You had no work permit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to school. I went to school. But because I didn't speak French, they kind of demoted me down to public school again, and myself and a Spanish kid that had fled Spain in the civil war in 1937. A year before we reached Belgium, they had a Spanish civil war. And this kid had fled Spain. And we were the tallest kid. That's very hard for me to be the tallest kid of anywhere. And we were number one and two in arithmetic. But for the rest, we didn't understand very much. But slowly, with comic books, I, I learned French. And by the time the Germans marched in, I could pass for Belgium. I spoke French fluently.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did September 1st, 1939, mean anything to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. We only found out via the newspapers. There was no Kristallnacht in, in, in Belgium. The Germans were not there yet. So there was no Kirstallnacht.

speaker: interviewer
text: They attacked Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1939. Yes, they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: In 1939. Did, did it have any effect on you whatsoever?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the effect it had on Belgium and France was that those countries had then declared war. France and, and Great Britain had declared war on Germany. We were at war, but nobody was fighting.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Belgian says, what's going on? This is-- they called it [NON-ENGLISH], which is the funny war. And then the Belgian says, long live war so that we can get killed, because this is boring. And I have to say that in 1940, they got their war. When did the Germans come into Belgium?

speaker: interviewee
text: May 10, 1940-- we woke up in the morning very early because the bombers had attacked the city. And the city was being bombed. And you heard planes circling and having dogfights in the sky and bombing the city. And we knew. The Belgian army bravely fought for 18 days till King Leopold ceased defending himself and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was fighting in the sky, the Germans and--

speaker: interviewee
text: German planes with Belgian planes, I guess. It was very hard for us to tell who's who. But we saw them circling each other. I also saw the British army march bravely up in the morning. And they were walking towards Waterloo. And the same afternoon or the next morning, they, they kind of rushed back home again towards Dunkirk. They, they had been recalled somehow and pulled back.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the Germans marched into Belgium, how did your life change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we couldn't go to school anymore. We now-- by that time, I was in a, a school that was run by the [INAUDIBLE]. It was kind of also where you learned a trade. And there were only Jewish refugees in that school. And we closed it immediately. And I continued to work for the Belgian proprietor of that shop. And I worked in that metal shop, brought home-- my, my dad had been arrested. All the, the males were being arrested by the Belgians now, because we were not Austrians anymore. We were Germans. Austria had ceased to exist. And the Belgians had arrestted all the males over 16 that were enemies, German nationals. And they first locked them up in a, in an armory. And I tried to get in there. I put on my scouts uniform, the Belgian scouts uniform. And I had a coat that they wanted to give my dad and some other, I guess, clothing. But they wouldn't let me in. I wasn't 16. I escaped by probably a few months. And then those people were transported by cattle cars, just like the Germans did, to southern France, to Saint Cyprien, where I had-- on the way down, somebody had marked the traitors, spies, on the cattle cars. And Senegalese troops started burning one of the cattle cars and shot into them, trying to kill those traitors. They were 90% Jews. Some of the guys died in those trains. And they finally reached this camp that had been used to sort out the Spaniards when they crossed the border in Saint Cyprien. It was built on sand. And the toilets were elevated like watchtowers with a barrel under them. And my dad had a book with him. And this book became-- every page could be sold. That was like gold. But I also know that you had to duck. Instead of going into the barrels, those pieces of paper were flying all over the camp. And you really had to duck in order not to get hit by one of the papers that had been used. Eventually with my uncle, who also was in that camp-- by the way, Erika's dad was in that camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was your father eventually freed?

speaker: interviewee
text: He wasn't freed. He escaped with fake papers. They went to Perpignan the next town in France. And they-- my uncle was-- obtained the permit to go back home because his wife was a Belgian. And then they went into a bank. And they faked the papers, saying that he was allowed to be in the company of my dad. And That paper is in the wall of the Holocaust Memorial in Montreal.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your family manage without your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, again, I brought home a paycheck from that metal work where I worked. And that's really all we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of laws did the Germans bring in against the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Belgium, the Germans didn't really bother with the Jews till 1942. They tried to educate the Belgians. And that, that photograph that you have of me and my sister standing in front of a poster was an effort to educate the Belgians that the Jews really are dangerous and that they have a conspiracy of all of Europe and that you really should watch out for them. It didn't work. If you look at this picture carefully, you will see a V for resist-- for, for victory, which was already applied by the resistance, the Belgian resistance. So the Belgians didn't help them, not like in France, where the [INAUDIBLE] police themselves arrested the Jews. So now the Germans imagined another kind of system where they, if they ask us to come, we had to come in and, and pick up a start at the high office of, of the German command. And I did that. I stood in line outside and got the star and had to give them my address. And two weeks later, you got a convocation to come and report yourself to the trains for labor in Germany. And half the Jewish population, the immigrant Jewish population in, in Brussels by themselves went and reported to the trains. Instead of going to, to work in Germany, of course, those trains went to Auschwitz. And only very few came back. If you did not report to those trains, you now, of course, broke the law. And you, you couldn't live in your apartment anymore now. You went into hiding like Anne Frank. And with the help of my, my school buddy whose father was a communist and the mother I had a wool shop-- she rented the premises next to her wool shop and was telling the landlord that she needed more space for her merchandise. And we moved in behind the store. And there was an apartment. And the shutters of that store was closed. And we only entered and exited our premises through her wool shop.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone else know about this hiding place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody except our family. My uncle knew. My uncle came to visit.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of this woman who helped you out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lebreun. And she was-- and, and Mrs. Absil, who eventually adopted me, was her aunt.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people lived in this little apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in our apartment, just my parents and my sister and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're talking about your hiding place now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who brought you food?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went out and bought food without wearing the star. At that point in time, I didn't have my fake ID yet. But yes, we did go out even though ID cards were stamped [NON-ENGLISH], Jew. We went out around the corner and bought newspapers and food.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would have happened if someone would have recognized you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well I once-- there was a traitor in, in Brussels. And he had worked for the Belgian police before the Germans occupied Brussels. His name was Jacques. I don't know his family name. And the Germans had found his records at the Belgian police headquarters. And they forced him to betray. He knew a lot of people because he was a bouncer in a, in an immigrant cafe. So he knew a lot of people. And they threatened to arrest his family. Or, or they promised to protect his family if he would help them identify Jews. I had never met Jacques because I had never been in an immigrant cafe. I went to school. But one day, I went to a swimming pool that was around the corner from where we lived with my dad. And my dad had stopped to buy a paper. And he did buy a German paper in that store. And due to the fact that he was behind me, a few steps behind me, and I saw Gestapo car. You could, you could tell. The low-slung front wheel drive Citroen were being used by the Gestapo. And I saw them go into a house, Gestapo in uniform. And I saw a Jewish guy stand in front of the car, looking at me. That was Jacques. But I didn't know who he was. And I had no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was dressed in overalls with jeans and a hat. And, and I looked him straight in the face. And he looked at me. And when I walked past him, he turned around. And I turned around. What's he want from me?

speaker: interviewee
text: And while he turned around looking at me, my dad passed him by. He probably would have arrested my dad. He didn't recognize me as a Jew. He wasn't sure. He, he doubted it. And because I was too stupid to be scared, I passed him by. Later on, when after the liberation, the resistance shot Jacques. I don't think he was dead. But he certainly was-- they shot at him. And he disappeared. The grave that people pointed out didn't seem to have his body. Nobody knows what happened to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you eventually have to leave your hiding place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the landlord that rented the second store to Mrs. Lebreun realized that she didn't use it for merchandise but that a family was hiding there. Somebody in the building probably gave us away. And he wouldn't prolong the lease.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we now found another little house that we rented all by ourselves. And we, we registered. You had to give the police one name of who was moving in there. And I believe they gave my sister's name, which was a mistake.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in that little house, in my absence and in my sister's absence, by accident, the Gestapo came to arrest my parents. That was in September, 1943. It was just one year before our liberation. And they went from there to Avenue Louise, the Gestapo headquarters in the, the cellar there. They went to the assembly camp of Malin on the outskirts of Brussels and left with the 22nd transport. The Belgians have exact records. And my parents are number three and four, prisoner number three and four. And they went to Auschwitz. The Germans told them that they are only going to a labor camp in Holland and that they'll be back soon. And my dad or my mother threw a letter from the train and a postcard. The letter is on the wall of the Holocaust center in Montreal. And it says, we are fine. I lost one of my shoes. Don't ask me how. And we are only going-- we're together with Belgian Jews. That was one of the first transport that also arrested Belgian Jews. And we are only going to Holland to a labor camp. And we'll see you soon. And bring the money to grandmother. That letter was thrown from a cattle car through a-- probably a slot between two [INAUDIBLE], through, through the wood. I'm sure that my parents didn't have any stamps. But the Belgians, with their bicycles, went along the railroad tracks and picked up the letters that prisoners threw from trains and mailed them. That letter was mailed to a girlfriend of my grandmother so that even if the Germans would find the letter, they wouldn't know where we are, like a safety precaution. They addressed this to a friend of my grandmother's.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your grandmother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandmother, due to the marriage, the famous marriage that I related to you-- I can tell you again-- was a Belgian subject. She was an old lady with white hair. And she lived in the little room all by herself. Nobody ever bothered her. And she lived in [INAUDIBLE], which is part of Brussels.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she die in Brussels?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She went back to Vienna. And the Viennese wouldn't, wouldn't pay her pension. Because she now was a Belgian subject. They wouldn't let her stay in Austria. So she went back to Belgium. She kind of lost all her papers. And with her birth certificate, she says, now I'm here. I'm an Austrian. I was born here. And she got a pension. She lost all her children in the war. She had three girls and two boys. And they all died before her. And all by herself, she went back to Vienna. And eventually, she fell and broke her hip and died at the age of 90.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Absil, you went back to your, you house. And your parents were gone.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who told you this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I rang the bell. And nobody opened the door till the, one neighbor that I had never seen before opened his door and says, please get away from here. The Germans just picked up your, your parents half, half an hour previously.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I walked to my uncle. I had no money in my pocket. And that night, my uncle and I, we broke into the house. We broke the seal of the Gestapo. And I picked up some clothes. And I picked up a little suit-- leather suitcase that was hidden in the garden. I still have it to this day.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in that suitcase were my parents' passports, a little bit of money, and some documents. And my dad, again, had been dabbling in leather goods. So we had a bunch of hides in the house. And I didn't want the Germans to take those hides. Don't ask me why. Those hides would have helped the war effort.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I dragged them up to the attic. And we had one of those windows that opened up this way. you know, one of those roof windows. And I threw the hides out. And I locked the window and blocked them so they wouldn't roll off. And I hoped that the rain would damage those hides.
